content:
# Genesis Chapter 5 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 3v–4r | **Source Image:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_005_page_001.jpg, GEN_005_page_002.jpg
**Additional Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) — LXX | **View Vaticanus online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209

---

**5:1** This is the book of the generations of Adam. On the day God created man, in the likeness of God he made him.

**5:2** Male and female he created them, and he blessed them and called their name Adam on the day they were created.

**5:3** And Adam lived one hundred and thirty years, and he fathered a son in his own likeness, according to his image, and he called his name Seth.

**5:4** And the days of Adam after he fathered Seth were eight hundred years, and he fathered sons and daughters.

**5:5** And all the days that Adam lived were nine hundred and thirty years, and he died.

**5:6** And Seth lived one hundred and five years, and he fathered Enosh.

**5:7** And Seth lived after he fathered Enosh eight hundred and seven years, and he fathered sons and daughters.

**5:8** And all the days of Seth were nine hundred and twelve years, and he died.

**5:9** And Enosh lived ninety years, and he fathered Kenan.

**5:10** And Enosh lived after he fathered Kenan eight hundred and fifteen years, and he fathered sons and daughters.

**5:11** And all the days of Enosh were nine hundred and five years, and he died.

**5:12** And Kenan lived seventy years, and he fathered Mahalalel.

**5:13** And Kenan lived after he fathered Mahalalel eight hundred and forty years, and he fathered sons and daughters.

**5:14** And all the days of Kenan were nine hundred and ten years, and he died.

**5:15** And Mahalalel lived sixty-five years, and he fathered Jared.

**5:16** And Mahalalel lived after he fathered Jared eight hundred and thirty years, and he fathered sons and daughters.

**5:17** And all the days of Mahalalel were eight hundred and ninety-five years, and he died.

**5:18** And Jared lived one hundred and sixty-two years, and he fathered Enoch.

**5:19** And Jared lived after he fathered Enoch eight hundred years, and he fathered sons and daughters.

**5:20** And all the days of Jared were nine hundred and sixty-two years, and he died.

**5:21** And Enoch lived sixty-five years, and he fathered Methuselah.

**5:22** And Enoch walked with God after he fathered Methuselah three hundred years, and he fathered sons and daughters.

**5:23** And all the days of Enoch were three hundred and sixty-five years.

**5:24** And Enoch walked with God, and he was not, for God took him.

**5:25** And Methuselah lived one hundred and eighty-seven years, and he fathered Lamech.

**5:26** And Methuselah lived after he fathered Lamech seven hundred and eighty-two years, and he fathered sons and daughters.

**5:27** And all the days of Methuselah were nine hundred and sixty-nine years, and he died.

**5:28** And Lamech lived one hundred and eighty-two years, and he fathered a son.

**5:29** And he called his name Noah, saying: "This one will bring us relief from our work and from the toil of our hands, from the ground that the LORD has cursed."

**5:30** And Lamech lived after he fathered Noah five hundred and ninety-five years, and he fathered sons and daughters.

**5:31** And all the days of Lamech were seven hundred and seventy-seven years, and he died.

**5:32** And Noah was five hundred years old, and Noah fathered Shem, Ham, and Japheth.

---

## SOPHIA's Arbiter Notes

### Manuscript Verification

Both manuscript images were examined directly.

**Page 001 (Folio 3v):** The Leningrad Codex page is photographed in mirror orientation (right-to-left Hebrew script reading direction confirmed). Three columns of Tiberian vocalized text are visible in characteristic Masoretic hand. The folio carries the close of Genesis 4 in its upper portion and the opening of the Genesis 5 toledot formula in the lower columns, consistent with the prior chapter's notes. The pe-parashah section marker is visible in the margin at the toledot heading. The divine name אֱלֹהִים (Elohim) is clearly written throughout the upper genealogical entries, with full Tiberian vowel pointing.

**Page 002 (Folio 4r):** The genealogy continues across three columns. The Enoch passage (vv. 22–24) is legible, with the Hitpael form וַיִּתְהַלֵּךְ visible. The Lamech speech of v. 29 is present, with the divine name יְהוָה (YHWH) appearing there and nowhere else in the chapter. The genealogy concludes at v. 32 and the text continues into Genesis 6 at the bottom of the folio. No lacunae or damage affecting the text of chapter 5 is visible on either folio.

---

### Arbitration Decisions

#### 5:1 — סֵפֶר (sefer): "book" vs. "scroll" vs. "record"

**EMET:** "scroll"
**LOGOS:** "book"
**PNEUMA:** "written account" (paraphrase, not word-for-word)

**Decision: "book."**

The Hebrew סֵפֶר (sefer) denotes a written document — its physical form is irrelevant to the English rendering. "Scroll" is archaeologically precise but anachronistic in English idiom; readers process "scroll" as a physical description rather than a document marker. "Book" is the KJV tradition (followed by ESV, NASB, NRSV) and captures the formality of the phrase: this is a document, a register, an official account of lineage. The LXX renders it βίβλος (biblos), which the New Testament echoes verbatim at Matthew 1:1 (βίβλος γενέσεως — "book of the generations"), establishing that βίβλος was already the authoritative rendering before the common era. PNEUMA's paraphrase "written account" captures the meaning but dissolves the single-word weight of סֵפֶר and breaks the formal three-word heading (sefer toledot Adam). "Book" is retained.

The consistency standard "generations" for toledot is applied throughout.

#### 5:2 — The Collective Naming of Adam

**EMET:** "he called their name Adam" — maintains the Hebrew literally, including the grammatically unusual plural pronoun with a singular name.
**LOGOS:** "called their name Adam" — same, with explanatory note on the collective force.
**PNEUMA:** "named them 'Humanity'" — interprets אָדָם as the generic "humankind," paraphrasing.

**Decision: "called their name Adam."**

PNEUMA's rendering "Humanity" is a valid interpretation of the semantic range of אָדָם, but it departs from the proper name that the text itself employs. The Hebrew is וַיִּקְרָא אֶת-שְׁמָם אָדָם — God "called their name Adam." The name אָדָם functions here simultaneously as personal name and generic humanity, but substituting "Humanity" requires translators to adjudicate a question the text holds open. Both EMET and LOGOS rightly preserve the proper name "Adam" as the collective designation. The word "their" (plural pronoun, singular name) is a Hebraism that English can carry. This reading also maintains the literary continuity: the genealogy of "Adam" in v. 1 begins with the naming of the collective "Adam" in v. 2, before narrowing in v. 3 to the individual patriarch.

#### 5:3 — The Reversed Order of tselem/demut

**EMET:** "in his likeness, according to his image" — demut first, tselem second (preserving MT order).
**LOGOS:** "in his own likeness, according to his image" — demut first, tselem second, with "his own" added for emphasis.
**PNEUMA:** "in his own likeness — after his own image" — demut first, tselem second.

**Decision: "in his own likeness, according to his image."**

All three agents correctly preserve the reversed order relative to Genesis 1:26 (where tselem precedes demut). This reversal is a deliberate literary signal: Adam transmits to Seth what was given to Adam, but the order is inverted — human procreation mirrors divine creation in reverse. The consensus translation adopts LOGOS's phrasing "in his own likeness, according to his image," including "his own" to accent the reflexive character of the transmission (Adam passing on Adam's likeness, not God's directly). This rendering:

- Preserves the required terminology: "image" for tselem (צֶלֶם), "likeness" for demut (דְּמוּת).
- Preserves the reversed order from 1:26.
- The addition of "his own" (not present as a separate word in Hebrew but implicit in the suffix -וֹ) appropriately flags that the possessive has shifted from God's "our" to Adam's "his."
- The prepositions are accurately represented: בְּ (be, "in") before demut; כְּ (ke, "according to") before tselem.

#### 5:24 — Enoch's Departure: "he was not" vs. "he was gone" vs. "he was no more"

**EMET:** "and-he-was-not" — a literal rendering of וְאֵינֶנּוּ.
**LOGOS:** "and he was not" — identical.
**PNEUMA:** "he was gone" — interpretive paraphrase.

**Decision: "and he was not."**

The Hebrew וְאֵינֶנּוּ (ve-einennu) is the negative existential אֵין with a third-person masculine singular suffix: literally, "and there-is-not-him," rendered in smooth English as "and he was not." This is the most formally precise English equivalent and is used by KJV, ESV, NASB. "He was no more" (NRSV, NIV) is equally defensible and marginally more natural in English, but "he was not" preserves the grammatical starkness of the Hebrew — the bare negation of existence — which is the literary point. PNEUMA's "he was gone" over-interprets: "gone" implies movement, direction, a going-away. The Hebrew simply negates his presence. He was not. The contrast with every other patriarch (who "died") is not softened by an explanation like "gone" or "no more."

The full verse reads: "And Enoch walked with God, and he was not, for God took him." The word "for" (כִּי, ki) introduces the reason for his non-being: God took him. The English preserves the causal conjunction.

#### 5:29 — Noah Wordplay: "comfort" vs. "relief" vs. "rest"

**EMET:** "comfort" (יְנַחֲמֵנוּ).
**LOGOS:** "bring us relief."
**PNEUMA:** "bring us relief."

**Decision: "bring us relief."**

The Hebrew verb is יְנַחֲמֵנוּ (yenachamenu), Piel of נחם (nacham) — "he will comfort/console us." The name נֹחַ (Noah) echoes this verb by sound; etymologically, Noah's root is נוּחַ (nuach, "to rest"), but the narrator exploits the phonological resonance between Noach and nacham. Neither "comfort" nor "rest" alone captures the full resonance. "Comfort" (EMET, KJV) tracks the literal nacham root but loses the nuach/rest dimension. "Rest" loses nacham entirely. "Relief" (LOGOS, PNEUMA) bridges both roots: relief from labor speaks of rest (nuach), and relief from suffering speaks of comfort (nacham). This is the standard choice of modern formal translations (NIV, NRSV) and the LXX's διαναπαύω ("to give complete rest") also leans toward rest rather than emotional comfort, further supporting "relief" over "comfort" alone.

The full verse follows LOGOS's phrasing closely: "This one will bring us relief from our work and from the toil of our hands, from the ground that the LORD has cursed." The reference to "the ground that the LORD has cursed" (הָאֲדָמָה אֲשֶׁר אֵרְרָהּ יְהוָה) is the only occurrence of the divine name יְהוָה in Genesis 5, embedded in Lamech's speech and recalling the curse of Genesis 3:17.

#### The "And He Died" Refrain — Format Decision

**EMET:** Inline at the end of the lifespan-total sentence: "...and he died [וַיָּמֹת]."
**LOGOS:** Inline at sentence end: "...and he died."
**PNEUMA:** Set apart on its own line, italicized or separated by dashes for rhetorical effect: "And then he died." — with horizontal rules between patriarchal entries.

**Decision: Inline, as part of the formula sentence; no horizontal rules between entries.**

PNEUMA's formatting is rhetorically powerful and effectively conveys the theological rhythm of the death refrain. However, the consensus bible maintains consistent formatting across agents and chapters. The formulaic architecture of Genesis 5 is already evident from the repetition of the same clauses; it does not require typographical dramatization. The inline placement — "and all the days of [Name] were [X] years, and he died" — preserves the formula exactly and matches the prose style established in GEN_001 through GEN_004. Verse numbers are retained as the primary structural markers.

The refrain וַיָּמֹת ("and he died") is rendered identically for all eight patriarchs: Adam, Seth, Enosh, Kenan, Mahalalel, Jared, Methuselah, and Lamech. Enoch alone lacks this ending (vv. 23–24). Noah does not receive it in this chapter.

---

### Names

All patriarchal names are rendered in their standard English-tradition spellings, consistent with the consensus translation's practice in prior chapters:

| Hebrew | Consensus | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| אָדָם | Adam | Personal name from v. 3 onward; collective in vv. 1–2 |
| שֵׁת | Seth | KJV/ESV tradition; EMET used "Shet" (not adopted) |
| אֱנוֹשׁ | Enosh | Universal among modern translations |
| קֵינָן | Kenan | KJV/ESV/NRSV standard; distinguishes from Cain (Qayin) |
| מַהֲלַלְאֵל | Mahalalel | Standard; meaning "praise of God" |
| יָרֶד | Jared | Standard; EMET used "Yered" (not adopted) |
| חֲנוֹךְ | Enoch | Standard; EMET used "Hanokh" (not adopted) |
| מְתוּשֶׁלַח | Methuselah | Standard |
| לֶמֶךְ | Lamech | Standard; different person from the Lamech of Genesis 4 |
| נֹחַ | Noah | Standard |
| שֵׁם חָם יָפֶת | Shem, Ham, and Japheth | Standard; EMET used "Yaphet" (not adopted) |

EMET's policy of rendering names in transliterated Hebrew form (Shet, Yered, Hanokh, Yaphet) is noted with respect. The consensus translation follows the established English tradition because the purpose of the consensus text is maximal accessibility while maintaining scholarly fidelity. Hebrew transliterations are preserved in the notes where they serve the reader.

---

### Structural and Theological Notes

**The toledot formula.** Genesis 5 opens with the second toledot formula of the book: זֶה סֵפֶר תּוֹלְדֹת אָדָם (zeh sefer toledot adam). It is the only toledot in Genesis prefaced by the word סֵפֶר (sefer, "book") — a document marker suggesting the narrator is invoking a written source, or at minimum that this genealogy is a formal written record. The literary unit extends from 5:1 through 6:8 (where the next toledot, "these are the generations of Noah," opens a new section).

**The image of God transmitted.** Verses 1–2 recapitulate Genesis 1:26–27 verbatim in several clauses, anchoring the genealogy in the creation account. The image of God (צֶלֶם, tselem) is not described as lost after the fall; it is transmitted forward. Adam begets Seth "in his own likeness, according to his image" (5:3), using both key terms from 1:26 but in reversed order (demut before tselem, not tselem before demut as in 1:26). The reversal is almost certainly deliberate: it marks that this is human transmission, not divine origination, while the presence of both terms insists on the continuity of the image-bearing line.

**The ten-patriarch structure.** Genesis 5 presents ten antediluvian patriarchs from Adam to Noah — a structure that parallels the ten postdiluvian patriarchs of Genesis 11:10–26 (Shem to Terah). Both ten-generation genealogies bracket the flood narrative. The structure also resonates with the Sumerian King List, which likewise presents ten antediluvian rulers with vastly inflated reigns culminating in a flood. The Genesis genealogy uses the same framework but transforms it: these are not kings legitimating dynasties but fathers transmitting the image of God.

**The death refrain.** The phrase וַיָּמֹת (vayyamot, "and he died") appears eight times: at the close of the lifespan summaries for Adam, Seth, Enosh, Kenan, Mahalalel, Jared, Methuselah, and Lamech. The cumulative weight of the refrain is the literary argument: death is the structural constant of post-fall human existence. Two figures do not receive it in this chapter — Enoch (who is taken by God) and Noah (who carries the narrative forward). The absence of the refrain at Enoch's entry is the theological climax of the chapter; it is all the more emphatic for being surrounded by eight instances of the formula on every side.

**Enoch's distinctive description.** Enoch alone is described as having "walked with God" (וַיִּתְהַלֵּךְ אֶת-הָאֱלֹהִים), and the phrase is repeated in both vv. 22 and 24. The verb הִתְהַלֵּךְ (hithallek) is the Hithpael of הָלַךְ (halak, "to walk"), carrying an iterative-habitual force: not a single occasion but a manner of life. The phrase "with God" (אֶת הָאֱלֹהִים) — with the definite article on אֱלֹהִים — is rare and emphatic. The same phrase is used of Noah at 6:9.

**The LXX Enoch.** The LXX (Codex Vaticanus) renders Enoch's characteristic description as εὐηρέστησεν Ἐνὼχ τῷ θεῷ — "Enoch was pleasing to God" (or "Enoch pleased God") — rather than "walked with God." The LXX also renders וְאֵינֶנּוּ as καὶ οὐχ εὑρίσκετο ("and he was not found") — implying that people searched for him — and uses μετατίθημι ("to transfer, translate") for לָקַח ("took"). This LXX vocabulary became the basis for Hebrews 11:5 ("By faith Enoch was taken up so that he should not see death, and he was not found, because God had taken him") and for the entire tradition of the "translation" of Enoch. The MT is followed in the consensus text.

**The divine name in Genesis 5.** The name אֱלֹהִים (Elohim, "God") is used exclusively in vv. 1–24. The name יְהוָה (YHWH, "the LORD") appears only once, at v. 29, embedded in Lamech's speech. This is consistent with the documentary-critical observation of a predominantly Elohistic vocabulary in the genealogy of chapter 5, contrasted with the Yahwistic material of chapters 2–4.

---

### LXX Chronology Table

The most significant divergence between the Masoretic Text (MT) and the LXX in all of Genesis occurs in the numerical data of Genesis 5. The LXX (Codex Vaticanus) consistently adds approximately 100 years to each patriarch's age at the birth of his firstborn son ("pre-firstborn age"), while reducing the post-firstborn years proportionally so that most total lifespans remain the same. The net effect is to push each birth event approximately a century later, expanding the pre-flood chronological framework.

| Patriarch | MT: Age at Firstborn | MT: Post-Firstborn | MT: Total | LXX: Age at Firstborn | LXX: Post-Firstborn | LXX: Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Adam | 130 | 800 | 930 | 230 | 700 | 930 |
| Seth | 105 | 807 | 912 | 205 | 707 | 912 |
| Enosh | 90 | 815 | 905 | 190 | 715 | 905 |
| Kenan | 70 | 840 | 910 | 170 | 740 | 910 |
| Mahalalel | 65 | 830 | 895 | 165 | 730 | 895 |
| Jared | 162 | 800 | 962 | 162 | 800 | 962 |
| Enoch | 65 | 300 | 365 | 165 | 200 | 365 |
| Methuselah | 187 | 782 | 969 | 167 | 802 | 969* |
| Lamech | 182 | 595 | 777 | 188 | 565 | 753 |
| Noah (at sons) | 500 | — | — | 500 | — | — |

*The Methuselah Problem: In the LXX as Vaticanus gives the numbers, Methuselah lives 969 years total, but the cumulative LXX chronology places his death approximately 14 years after the flood — an acknowledged internal inconsistency. The MT chronology does not have this problem: Methuselah dies in the year of the flood (a traditional rabbinic observation). Some LXX manuscripts give 187 (matching MT) rather than 167 for Methuselah's pre-firstborn age, resolving the inconsistency; Vaticanus reads 167.

**The +100 pattern** applies consistently to Adam through Mahalalel and to Enoch, but not to Jared (unchanged) or Methuselah (actually slightly reduced). The Lamech variant (+6 in LXX: 188 vs. MT's 182) does not follow the +100 pattern and may reflect an independent textual tradition. Three principal scholarly explanations exist for the divergence: (1) the LXX reflects an independent Hebrew Vorlage with different numbers; (2) the LXX translator or a later editor deliberately expanded the pre-firstborn ages for chronological harmonization; (3) the proto-MT tradition deliberately reduced the figures to avoid messianic calculation or to distinguish Israelite chronology from Babylonian antediluvian king-list figures. The text-critical evidence does not resolve the question definitively. The MT is followed throughout this translation.

---

*Signed: SOPHIA (Σοφία — Wisdom)*
*Consensus arbiter of EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA.*
*Translation from Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 3v–4r, photographed by the West Semitic Research Project (© Bruce E. Zuckerman).*
*LXX consultation: Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana.*
